summary: The q3 dataset contains 10 problems (q1-q10), each showing exactly 100 issues with no proof status differentiation available. The uniform issue count and absence of resolution metadata prevent meaningful traction or solvability analysis, indicating the dataset context is incomplete or aggregated at a placeholder level.
problems: All 10 problems report an identical 100 issues each, suggesting a capped, truncated, or placeholder metric rather than real per-problem activity data
No proof status breakdown (open vs. resolved, in-progress) is provided, making it impossible to assess which problems are near completion
No timestamps, contributor counts, or issue categories are available to identify genuine activity or traction
The 'Issues Per Problem' section header is present but empty, and 'Proof Status' only lists raw counts without states
highlights: Complete coverage: all 10 problems (q1-q10) are tracked with consistent metric reporting
Uniform 100-issue ceiling implies a structured collection pipeline is in place and capturing data for every problem
Dataset is well-scoped at a manageable 10 problems, making targeted manual review feasible

suggested_todos.title: Investigate why every problem reports exactly 100 issues — likely a query cap or pagination limit
Add proof status states (open/in-progress/resolved) to enable solvability and traction analysis
Enrich issue records with timestamps and contributor metadata to surface real activity trends
Manually spot-check 2-3 problems (e.g., q1, q5, q10) to validate data integrity against the dashboard
Define and document a clear 'solvable soon' heuristic once status data is available
